subtopic_detail: Functional neurological vs malingering vs factitious
stem: You are the neurology registrar reviewing a 38-year-old woman with sudden onset right leg weakness for 4 weeks. Examination shows give-way weakness with positive Hoover sign and intact reflexes. MRI brain and spine are normal. She is genuinely distressed by the symptoms and wants help. She does not seek work cover and has no pending litigation. She is not a healthcare worker. She has a remote history of anxiety. She tells you the leg moves freely when she is distracted reaching for her drink bottle.

data_table.Examination: Right leg 4/5 give-way weakness, positive Hoover, normal reflexes, normal tone, normal sensation in non-anatomical distribution
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain and spine: nil acute; nerve conduction studies normal

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?

options.text: Functional neurological disorder with positive Hoover sign supporting the diagnosis
options.rationale: Functional neurological disorder is now a rule-in diagnosis based on positive clinical signs (Hoover, distractibility, give-way weakness) rather than exclusion; the patient has genuine symptoms and seeks help, distinguishing from factitious and malingering. (Source: RACP Position FND; DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Factitious disorder imposed on self with intentional production of weakness for sick role
options.rationale: Factitious disorder requires conscious symptom production motivated by the sick role; the patient is genuinely distressed and lacks evidence of deception. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Malingering with intentional symptom production motivated by an external incentive
options.rationale: Malingering requires identifiable external incentive (financial, legal, work avoidance); none is present in this case. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Somatic symptom disorder predominantly characterised by pain in absence of weakness
options.rationale: Somatic symptom disorder centres on excessive thoughts, feelings or behaviours about somatic symptoms; the dominant feature here is the motor deficit with positive functional signs. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Multiple sclerosis with normal initial imaging that requires repeat MRI in three months
options.rationale: MS is unlikely with normal MRI, intact reflexes, give-way weakness and positive Hoover; functional signs are specific rule-in features. (Source: McDonald Criteria 2024)

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder (FND) is the contemporary term for what was previously called conversion disorder. DSM-5-TR reframed it as a rule-in diagnosis based on positive clinical features incompatible with neurological disease, not a diagnosis of exclusion. Positive features include Hoover sign, hip abductor sign, tremor entrainment, give-way weakness, distractibility, and seizure features inconsistent with electroclinical correlation. The patient has genuine symptoms and distress, and motivation is not consciously to assume the sick role (factitious) or to obtain external gain (malingering). Treatment combines clear positive communication of the diagnosis, physiotherapy with FND-trained therapists, and addressing predisposing and perpetuating psychological factors. Misdiagnosis rate of FND on long-term follow-up is approximately 4%.
key_points: FND = rule-in diagnosis from positive signs (Hoover, distractibility, give-way)
Factitious = conscious symptom production for sick role
Malingering = conscious symptom production for external incentive
Long-term misdiagnosis rate of FND ~4% (no higher than other neuro disorders)
Treatment: clear positive communication + FND-trained physiotherapy
explanation.pearls: Hoover sign has sensitivity around 63% and specificity around 100% for functional leg weakness, making it the single highest-yield bedside test.

stem: You are the paediatric medicine registrar admitting a 16-year-old girl with anorexia nervosa for medical stabilisation. Her BMI is 14.2 with weight loss of 25% over six months. She has been eating <500 kcal per day for four weeks. She is bradycardic and hypotensive. The dietitian plans to start nasogastric feeds at 1200 kcal per day, increasing by 200 kcal every 48 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 88/52 supine, HR 42 supine, T 35.8, RR 14
data_table.Examination: Cachectic, lanugo, cold peripheries, no peripheral oedema
data_table.Investigations: Na 138, K 3.4, phosphate 0.95, magnesium 0.74, glucose 3.6, LFTs mildly deranged

lead_in: Which electrolyte requires the most frequent monitoring during the first 5 days of refeeding?

options.text: Sodium, with twice daily measurement to avoid central pontine myelinolysis from rapid correction
options.rationale: Sodium derangements occur but are not the primary driver of refeeding cardiac events; phosphate is. (Source: ANZAED Refeeding Guideline)

options.text: Phosphate, with daily measurement and proactive supplementation as required
options.rationale: Hypophosphataemia is the hallmark of refeeding syndrome and drives cardiac arrhythmia and respiratory failure; daily monitoring with proactive supplementation is the cornerstone of safe refeeding. (Source: ANZAED Refeeding Guideline)

options.text: Potassium, with three times daily measurement to maintain a level above 4.0 mmol/L
options.rationale: Hypokalaemia and hypomagnesaemia also occur in refeeding but phosphate is the marker most predictive of refeeding syndrome severity. (Source: ANZAED Refeeding Guideline)

options.text: Magnesium, with twice daily measurement to maintain a level above 0.85 mmol/L target
options.rationale: Magnesium is monitored but is not the most predictive marker; phosphate is the single most important electrolyte during early refeeding. (Source: ANZAED Refeeding Guideline)

options.text: Bicarbonate, with daily venous gas to monitor for refeeding-induced lactic acidosis early
options.rationale: Bicarbonate trend is informative but is not the primary monitored parameter; refeeding lactic acidosis is rare compared with refeeding hypophosphataemia. (Source: ANZAED Refeeding Guideline)

explanation.summary: Refeeding syndrome is driven by an insulin surge when carbohydrate is reintroduced after starvation, shifting phosphate, magnesium and potassium intracellularly. Hypophosphataemia is the hallmark electrolyte disturbance and the strongest predictor of cardiac and respiratory complications. Australian Eating Disorder guidelines (ANZAED) recommend daily phosphate, magnesium, potassium and glucose for the first 5 to 7 days, with proactive phosphate supplementation if levels fall below 0.85 mmol/L or rapidly trend down. Thiamine should be given before any carbohydrate to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Cardiac monitoring is recommended for the first 72 hours in severely malnourished patients. ECG should be repeated daily until refeeding is established.
key_points: Refeeding syndrome: insulin-driven intracellular shift of PO4, K, Mg
Hypophosphataemia is the hallmark and strongest mortality predictor
ANZAED: daily PO4, K, Mg, glucose for first 5-7 days
Give thiamine 100 mg IV before any carbohydrate (Wernicke prevention)
Cardiac monitoring for first 72 hours if severely malnourished
explanation.pearls: Serum phosphate below 0.5 mmol/L during refeeding carries a 30% risk of cardiac arrhythmia and warrants IV replacement (e.g. potassium phosphate 20 mmol over 6 hours) rather than oral.

stem: You are the community mental health registrar seeing a 52-year-old man, brought in by his daughter. For 18 months he has been convinced his neighbour is poisoning his garden vegetables and has been writing letters to the council. He has continued working as a successful accountant throughout. He denies hallucinations and his speech is organised. His self-care and occupational function are intact. There is no mood episode. There is no substance use. He has no first-rank symptoms of schizophrenia.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: organised speech, persecutory delusion of garden poisoning, no hallucinations, no thought disorder
data_table.Investigations: FBC, EUC, TSH, B12, syphilis serology, CT brain normal

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder with single persecutory delusion lasting beyond one month
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder is by definition <1 month duration; 18 months excludes this diagnosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Schizophrenia paranoid type with preserved cognitive function on testing
options.rationale: Schizophrenia requires criterion A symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, disorganised speech, grossly disorganised behaviour or negative symptoms) for >=1 month with functional decline >=6 months; preserved high-level work function and isolated delusion does not fit. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Delusional disorder persecutory type with preserved psychosocial function
options.rationale: Encapsulated non-bizarre delusion >1 month, preserved function outside the delusion, no prominent hallucinations and absence of mood episode satisfies DSM-5-TR delusional disorder criteria. (Source: DSM-5-TR; RANZCP CPG Schizophrenia)

options.text: Late-onset schizophrenia with negative symptoms predominating over positive symptoms
options.rationale: Late-onset schizophrenia features prominent positive symptoms and functional decline; this patient has neither functional decline nor multiple criterion A symptoms. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Substance-induced psychotic disorder with persistent residual persecutory beliefs
options.rationale: There is no history of substance use; substance-induced psychotic disorder requires temporal association with intoxication or withdrawal. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: Delusional disorder is characterised by one or more delusions for at least 1 month, in the absence of other criterion A symptoms of schizophrenia, preserved psychosocial function outside the delusion, and no prominent hallucinations. Subtypes include persecutory (most common), erotomanic, grandiose, jealous, somatic, and mixed. Mean age of onset is later than schizophrenia (40s to 50s). Treatment combines antipsychotic medication (response rates 30 to 50%) with engagement-focused psychotherapy. Distinguishing features from schizophrenia are absence of negative symptoms, preserved function and encapsulated delusion. Excluding organic causes (frontal lobe lesion, B12 deficiency, neurosyphilis) is mandatory.
key_points: Delusional disorder = >=1 delusion, >=1 month, preserved function, no other criterion A
Onset typically 40s-50s vs schizophrenia onset late teens to early 20s
Persecutory subtype is most common
Response to antipsychotic 30-50%; engagement is the limiting factor
Always exclude organic cause (frontal lesion, B12, neurosyphilis)
explanation.pearls: Delusional disorder shared with a close contact (folie à deux) is now coded under 'Other Specified Schizophrenia Spectrum and Other Psychotic Disorder' in DSM-5-TR rather than as a separate entity.

stem: You are the GP registrar seeing a 36-year-old man with PTSD following a workplace assault 6 months ago. He has intrusive memories, avoidance of the workplace, hypervigilance and nightmares. He has not yet had any trial of psychotherapy or pharmacotherapy. He has no comorbid alcohol use, no dissociative symptoms and no suicidal ideation. He prefers an evidence-based approach without medication initially.

data_table.Examination: Mental state: anxious, organised, no SI, no dissociation

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line psychotherapy to recommend?

options.text: Dialectical behaviour therapy with skills training group for emotion dysregulation in PTSD
options.rationale: DBT is first-line for borderline personality disorder; in uncomplicated PTSD without comorbid BPD, trauma-focused therapy is preferred first. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Supportive counselling weekly with relaxation training for hypervigilance and arousal symptoms
options.rationale: Generic supportive counselling is not first-line for PTSD; trauma-focused therapy demonstrates superior outcomes for PTSD symptom reduction. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Critical incident stress debriefing in a single session focused on the recent workplace event
options.rationale: Single-session debriefing is not recommended and may worsen outcomes; trauma-focused therapy delivered over multiple sessions is preferred. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy with prolonged exposure components over 8 to 12 weeks
options.rationale: Australian PTSD guidelines (Phoenix Australia / NHMRC) endorse trauma-focused CBT and EMDR as first-line for adult PTSD, with TF-CBT having the largest evidence base; EMDR is an equally valid alternative. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Mindfulness-based stress reduction with somatic experiencing as primary trauma-focused therapy
options.rationale: Mindfulness and somatic experiencing have weaker evidence and are second-line; TF-CBT and EMDR are first-line. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Australian PTSD treatment guidelines (Phoenix Australia, endorsed by RANZCP and NHMRC) place trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy (which includes prolonged exposure and cognitive processing therapy variants) and eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing (EMDR) as first-line psychotherapies for adult PTSD. SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) and SNRIs (venlafaxine) are first-line pharmacotherapy and can be combined with trauma-focused therapy when symptoms are severe or in patient preference. Single-session debriefing is not recommended. Adjunctive prazosin can reduce trauma-related nightmares. Comorbidity (depression, substance use) often co-exists and may need parallel treatment. NDIS and workers compensation pathways may apply in workplace-related PTSD.
key_points: TF-CBT (prolonged exposure, cognitive processing) = first-line
EMDR = equally effective first-line alternative
SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) and SNRIs (venlafaxine) first-line pharmacotherapy
Avoid single-session critical incident debriefing
Prazosin can be added for persistent nightmares
explanation.pearls: Prazosin 1 to 6 mg at night reduces PTSD-related nightmares with NNT around 4; titrate from 1 mg, monitor for orthostatic hypotension.

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar starting a 42-year-old man on lisdexamfetamine for adult ADHD confirmed at multi-source assessment. He has a 10 year history of essential hypertension on amlodipine, with last clinic BP 138/84. He has no history of ischaemic heart disease, arrhythmia or stroke. His ECG is unremarkable. His brother had sudden cardiac death at age 38 with no autopsy diagnosis. He has stable employment as a long-distance truck driver requiring an unrestricted commercial driver's licence.

data_table.Investigations: ECG: sinus rhythm 78, QTc 416 ms, no LVH, no ischaemic change

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step before starting lisdexamfetamine?

options.text: Start lisdexamfetamine 30 mg daily and monitor BP and HR at 2 weeks, 4 weeks, then 3 monthly
options.rationale: Family history of unexplained sudden cardiac death under 40 requires cardiology review before stimulant initiation, regardless of normal baseline ECG. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline 2024)

options.text: Request transthoracic echocardiogram and 24 hour Holter monitor then start stimulant if normal
options.rationale: Ordering investigations without cardiology input for inherited arrhythmia evaluation is suboptimal; cardiology review directs appropriate testing including genetic considerations. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline 2024)

options.text: Switch amlodipine to perindopril and recheck BP and HR in two weeks before starting stimulant
options.rationale: Antihypertensive switch is not the limiting factor; cardiology review for family history is the gating step before stimulant initiation. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline 2024)

options.text: Start atomoxetine 40 mg daily instead and avoid all stimulants in commercial truck drivers
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is a reasonable alternative but commercial driving status alone is not a contraindication to stimulants; cardiology review for family history is the appropriate immediate step. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline 2024)

options.text: Refer to cardiology for review of unexplained sudden cardiac death in first-degree relative
options.rationale: First-degree relative with unexplained sudden cardiac death under 40 is a flag for inherited cardiac conditions (long QT, HCM, ARVC) and warrants cardiology workup before stimulant initiation per Australian ADHD Guideline 2024. (Source: Australian ADHD Guideline 2024)

explanation.summary: Australian ADHD Guideline 2024 (AADPA) recommends cardiovascular history, examination and baseline BP/HR before stimulant initiation in adults. Routine ECG is not mandatory in the absence of red flags, but red flags trigger cardiology review and further investigation. Red flags include syncope on exertion, palpitations on exertion, known structural heart disease, family history of sudden cardiac death under age 40, family history of cardiomyopathy or channelopathy, and inadequately controlled hypertension. Stimulants typically raise BP by 2 to 5 mmHg and HR by 3 to 7 bpm. Monitoring after initiation includes BP and HR at 2 weeks, 4 weeks, 3 months, then 6 monthly. Driving authority disclosure follows state rules (DVA in NT, RMS NSW, VicRoads, TMR Qld).
key_points: Cardiac history + examination + BP/HR baseline before stimulant
Routine ECG not required in absence of red flags
First-degree SCD under 40 = cardiology review before starting
Stimulants typically raise BP 2-5 mmHg and HR 3-7 bpm
Disclose to driving authority per state rules (e.g. AustRoads)
explanation.pearls: Lisdexamfetamine vs immediate-release dexamphetamine: lisdexamfetamine is a prodrug requiring red-cell esterase cleavage, giving a smoother 10 to 12 hour profile and lower abuse potential, which favours it in adult ADHD.

stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar called to ICU for a 28-year-old man with schizophrenia. He was admitted three days ago with acute psychosis and started on olanzapine 10 mg daily. Twelve hours ago he became mute and immobile with rigid posturing and waxy flexibility, then over the past six hours has developed fever and autonomic instability. He had a similar episode of mutism and posturing two years ago that responded to lorazepam. Total dose of antipsychotic in this admission has been 30 mg olanzapine in divided doses. CK has risen from 180 to 3200 over 12 hours.

data_table.Vital signs: BP fluctuating 92/56 to 168/102, HR 124, T 39.6, RR 28, SpO2 96% RA
data_table.Examination: Mute, posturing, lead-pipe rigidity, waxy flexibility, no clonus, no hyperreflexia
data_table.Investigations: CK 3200 (rising), WCC 14.8, CRP 26, EUC: Cr 132, K 4.8, lactate 3.2; venous gas: pH 7.30, HCO3 18; urine myoglobin positive
data_table.Medications: Olanzapine 10 mg daily x 3 days (total 30 mg this admission)

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next intervention?

options.text: Stop olanzapine and arrange urgent electroconvulsive therapy with IV fluids and supportive care
options.rationale: Malignant catatonia and NMS overlap clinically and pathophysiologically; in a patient with prior lorazepam-responsive catatonia who now has rigidity, fever, autonomic instability and elevated CK, ECT is the indicated first-line treatment after stopping antipsychotic, with mortality reduction over pharmacotherapy alone. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Continue olanzapine at reduced dose and start IV dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg with bromocriptine
options.rationale: Continuing antipsychotic in suspected NMS or malignant catatonia is contraindicated; dantrolene + bromocriptine have weak evidence and do not replace ECT for malignant catatonia. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Stop olanzapine and trial IV lorazepam 4 mg now with planned escalation to 16 mg over 24 hours
options.rationale: High-dose benzodiazepine alone in malignant catatonia with autonomic instability is unlikely to reverse the syndrome promptly enough; ECT is the definitive treatment with the lowest mortality. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position)

options.text: Stop olanzapine and switch to quetiapine 100 mg daily once afebrile and CK falling consistently
options.rationale: Reintroducing any antipsychotic before complete resolution of malignant catatonia or NMS risks recurrence and death; antipsychotic rechallenge must wait >=2 weeks after full resolution. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Continue current management and observe in ICU for 24 hours before deciding on ECT referral
options.rationale: Delaying definitive treatment of malignant catatonia or NMS increases mortality substantially; ECT should be expedited within 24 to 48 hours of recognition. (Source: RANZCP Catatonia Position)

explanation.summary: Malignant catatonia and neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) are now widely considered overlapping conditions on a single spectrum, with shared features of rigidity, hyperthermia, autonomic instability, altered consciousness and elevated CK. The temporal association with antipsychotic, presence of lead-pipe rigidity, and rapid rise in CK favour NMS as the proximate trigger, while prior lorazepam-responsive catatonia favours an underlying catatonic substrate. Management is identical: cease the offending antipsychotic, aggressive supportive care (cooling, IV fluids, monitoring for rhabdomyolysis-induced AKI), and definitive treatment with ECT, particularly when there is autonomic instability or failure to respond to high-dose benzodiazepine. Bromocriptine and dantrolene have limited evidence and are second-line adjuncts. Antipsychotic rechallenge must be delayed by at least 2 weeks after full resolution and is usually with a lower-potency D2 antagonist or a partial agonist.
key_points: Malignant catatonia and NMS overlap clinically and pathophysiologically
Stop offending antipsychotic; aggressive supportive care; monitor for AKI
ECT is the definitive treatment; bromocriptine/dantrolene weakly supported
Antipsychotic rechallenge >=2 weeks after resolution
Prior catatonia response to lorazepam predicts catatonic substrate
explanation.pearls: ECT for malignant catatonia or NMS typically requires daily or bilateral treatments rather than the standard 3-times-weekly schedule, with first response often seen after 3 to 6 treatments.

subtopic_detail: Pre-eclampsia BP threshold and drug choice
stem: You are the obstetrics registrar reviewing a 32-year-old primigravida at 33+2 weeks of gestation with BP 162/106 on two readings 15 minutes apart. She has new-onset proteinuria of 1+ on urinalysis and a headache, with no visual disturbance or epigastric pain. Reflexes are not pathologically brisk. CTG is reassuring. Her booking BP was 110/70 and no prior antihypertensive history.

data_table.Examination: Headache, no visual changes, no epigastric pain, no clonus
data_table.Investigations: Urinalysis: protein 1+, PCR pending; FBC normal; LFTs normal; uric acid 0.38; platelets 218

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate antihypertensive choice?

options.text: Oral nifedipine immediate-release 10 mg sublingually with repeat BP at 30 minutes
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine is unpredictable and unsafe in pregnancy; oral nifedipine PA tablets or labetalol are preferred. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Oral labetalol 200 mg with repeat BP at 30 minutes and escalate if not below target
options.rationale: SOMANZ recommends oral labetalol as a first-line antihypertensive in severe pregnancy hypertension when oral therapy is feasible; target BP <160/110 acutely, then <140/90 ongoing. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Sublingual captopril 25 mg with repeat BP at 30 minutes and consider escalation if uncontrolled
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy because of foetal renal injury and oligohydramnios; never used in pre-eclampsia management. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 4 g loading dose with immediate transfer to a labour ward for delivery
options.rationale: Magnesium is indicated for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia or for eclampsia treatment, but is not an antihypertensive; antihypertensive control is the priority. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023)

options.text: Oral methyldopa 250 mg twice daily as a first-line oral agent in severe pregnancy hypertension
options.rationale: Methyldopa has slow onset (3-6 hours) and is used for ongoing rather than acute severe hypertension control. (Source: SOMANZ Hypertension in Pregnancy 2023)

explanation.summary: SOMANZ defines severe hypertension in pregnancy as systolic >=160 or diastolic >=110 mmHg, requiring urgent pharmacological control. Acute first-line oral options are labetalol or nifedipine PA; methyldopa has slow onset and is reserved for ongoing control. IV options include hydralazine and labetalol. Target acute reduction is to <160/110 within 30 minutes, then ongoing target <140/90. ACE inhibitors and ARBs are contraindicated in pregnancy. Magnesium sulfate (loading 4 g IV over 20 minutes, then 1 g/hour) is indicated for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia and for eclampsia treatment. Delivery is the only definitive treatment and is timed by gestation, maternal stability, and foetal wellbeing.
key_points: Severe pregnancy HTN >=160/110: urgent treatment within 30 min
Oral labetalol, oral nifedipine PA = first-line
IV labetalol or hydralazine if oral not feasible
Avoid ACEi/ARBs in pregnancy (foetal renal injury)
Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis in severe PE; not for BP control
explanation.pearls: Magnesium sulfate loading is 4 g IV over 20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour maintenance; serum levels are not routinely required if reflexes are monitored 2 hourly.

stem: You are the GP shared-care doctor for a 31-year-old G2P1 at 26 weeks of gestation. She has no diabetes risk factors apart from a BMI of 31 at booking and a previous baby weighing 4150 g. Her early pregnancy fasting glucose at 11 weeks was 4.6 mmol/L. She would like to know what GDM screening she should have now.

data_table.Investigations: Early pregnancy fasting glucose 4.6; HbA1c not done; recent urinalysis nil

lead_in: What is the most appropriate GDM screening test now?

options.text: HbA1c with a diagnostic threshold of 6.5% as the screening test of choice at 24 to 28 weeks
options.rationale: HbA1c is unreliable in pregnancy due to increased red cell turnover and is not the OGTT-equivalent diagnostic test for GDM in Australia. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guideline)

options.text: Repeat fasting glucose alone with same 5.1 mmol/L cut-off as in early pregnancy screening
options.rationale: Fasting glucose alone misses post-prandial GDM phenotype; the OGTT detects all three diagnostic time points. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guideline)

options.text: 75 g 2-hour oral glucose tolerance test with fasting and 1 and 2 hour samples taken
options.rationale: ADIPS recommends 75 g 2-hour OGTT at 24 to 28 weeks for all pregnant women, with fasting, 1 hour and 2 hour samples; diagnostic thresholds are 5.1, 10.0 and 8.5 mmol/L respectively. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guideline)

options.text: 50 g 1-hour glucose challenge test followed by 100 g 3-hour OGTT if positive at 7.8 mmol/L
options.rationale: The two-step 50 g GCT then 100 g OGTT is a US protocol; Australia uses the one-step 75 g 2-hour OGTT. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guideline)

options.text: Random capillary glucose with a 7.8 mmol/L cut-off and reflex to a confirmatory test if positive
options.rationale: Random capillary glucose is not the screening test for GDM in Australian guidelines. (Source: ADIPS GDM Guideline)

explanation.summary: Australian Diabetes in Pregnancy Society (ADIPS) recommends universal screening for GDM with a 75 g 2-hour OGTT at 24 to 28 weeks. The diagnostic thresholds are fasting >=5.1, 1 hour >=10.0, or 2 hour >=8.5 mmol/L. Any single value meeting the threshold confirms GDM. Early-pregnancy screening at booking with fasting glucose or HbA1c targets women at high risk (prior GDM, BMI >=35, polycystic ovary syndrome, family history, ethnic risk groups). HbA1c >=6.5% at booking suggests overt diabetes rather than GDM. Initial management is diet and exercise with self-monitoring of blood glucose; metformin and/or insulin are added if targets not met. Foetal growth scans at 28 to 32 weeks and aspirin for pre-eclampsia prophylaxis as appropriate.
key_points: ADIPS: universal 75 g 2-hour OGTT at 24-28 weeks
Diagnostic thresholds: fasting >=5.1, 1h >=10.0, 2h >=8.5 mmol/L
Any single value meeting threshold = GDM
Booking HbA1c >=6.5% suggests overt diabetes
Initial management: diet + exercise + SMBG; escalate to metformin/insulin
explanation.pearls: Australian GDM treatment targets are fasting <5.0 mmol/L and 2 hour post-meal <6.7 mmol/L, tighter than non-pregnant diabetes targets and based on the HAPI study.

stem: You are the antenatal clinic registrar reviewing a 26-year-old G1 at 28+0 weeks with blood group O Rh negative and negative antibody screen. The booking ultrasound and 20-week morphology scan were normal. Her partner is Rh positive but the foetal Rh status is not known. There has been no antepartum bleeding or invasive procedure to date. She is well.

data_table.Investigations: Blood group O Rh negative; antibody screen negative; FBC normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Defer anti-D until evidence of sensitising event such as bleeding or amniocentesis arises
options.rationale: Routine antenatal anti-D prevents silent sensitisation by foetomaternal haemorrhage which can occur without overt bleeding; deferral risks anti-D sensitisation. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guideline)

options.text: Administer 1500 IU anti-D immunoglobulin intramuscularly at 28 weeks only with no further dose
options.rationale: The Australian dose is 625 IU at 28 and 34 weeks (total 1250 IU); a single 1500 IU dose is the UK regimen and not Australian standard. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guideline)

options.text: Administer 250 IU anti-D immunoglobulin intramuscularly now and reassess at 34 weeks for further
options.rationale: 250 IU is the dose for first-trimester sensitising events, not routine antenatal prophylaxis. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guideline)

options.text: Administer 625 IU anti-D immunoglobulin intramuscularly at 28 weeks and again at 34 weeks
options.rationale: NBA guidelines recommend routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis at 28 and 34 weeks for Rh negative women with negative antibody screen, with 625 IU IM each dose; this regimen reduces sensitisation from 1.5% to <0.2%. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guideline)

options.text: Test foetal Rh status by maternal cell-free DNA and only treat if foetus confirmed Rh positive
options.rationale: Cell-free foetal DNA Rh typing is available in some Australian centres but is not yet universal; in standard NBA pathway, all Rh negative women receive routine anti-D. (Source: NBA Anti-D Guideline)

explanation.summary: Routine antenatal anti-D immunoglobulin prophylaxis reduces D-antigen sensitisation in Rh-negative women from approximately 1.5% to less than 0.2%. The Australian National Blood Authority regimen is 625 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks for women with negative antibody screen. Additional doses (250 IU first trimester, 625 IU second/third trimester) are required for potentially sensitising events including miscarriage, antepartum haemorrhage, amniocentesis, chorionic villus sampling, external cephalic version, and abdominal trauma. Postpartum anti-D 625 IU is given within 72 hours of delivery if the baby is confirmed Rh positive. Kleihauer testing quantifies foetomaternal haemorrhage and guides additional anti-D dosing when feto-maternal haemorrhage exceeds 4 mL.
key_points: Australian routine antenatal anti-D: 625 IU IM at 28 and 34 weeks
Sensitising events <12 weeks: 250 IU; >=12 weeks: 625 IU within 72 hours
Postpartum 625 IU within 72 hours if baby Rh positive
Kleihauer test quantifies FMH; additional anti-D if FMH >4 mL
cffDNA Rh typing is emerging but not universal in Australia yet
explanation.pearls: 625 IU of Rh(D) immunoglobulin neutralises approximately 6 mL of D-positive foetal red cells; Kleihauer test estimates FMH and dictates whether additional doses are required.

stem: You are the obstetrics registrar admitting a 28-year-old G2P1 at 30+1 weeks with confirmed PPROM by sterile speculum showing pooling and positive amniSure. She has no contractions, no abdominal pain, no fever and no bleeding. The CTG is reassuring. Foetal growth is appropriate for gestation. She has no penicillin allergy.

data_table.Examination: Pooling of clear liquor in posterior fornix; cervix appears closed on speculum
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal; CRP 4; group B Strep culture pending; foetal fibronectin not done

lead_in: Which combination of immediate interventions is most appropriate?

options.text: Tocolysis with nifedipine for 48 hours and discharge home with weekly outpatient review
options.rationale: Tocolysis is not recommended in PPROM as it does not improve outcomes and may delay recognition of chorioamnionitis; inpatient management is standard. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM with broad-spectrum coverage by IV ampicillin and IV gentamicin
options.rationale: Empirical IV ampicillin + gentamicin is for clinical chorioamnionitis (fever, tachycardia, uterine tenderness); in stable PPROM, oral erythromycin is the evidence-based latency antibiotic. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM with amoxicillin and clavulanic acid orally for 7 days as cover
options.rationale: Co-amoxiclav in PPROM is associated with increased risk of neonatal necrotising enterocolitis (ORACLE I) and is contraindicated as latency antibiotic. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Magnesium sulfate neuroprotection only with planned delivery within 24 hours of diagnosis
options.rationale: Magnesium neuroprotection is indicated when delivery is imminent (within 24 hours) <30 weeks but is not the sole intervention here; corticosteroid and antibiotic latency are also indicated. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Guideline)

options.text: Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM with erythromycin 250 mg orally four times daily for 10 days
options.rationale: RANZCOG and SOMANZ recommend antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM at 0 and 24 hours) and 10 days of erythromycin in PPROM <34 weeks to reduce respiratory distress, intraventricular haemorrhage and chorioamnionitis. (Source: RANZCOG PPROM Guideline)

explanation.summary: PPROM at 24 to 34 weeks is managed expectantly with inpatient monitoring, antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM 24 hours apart x 2), and latency antibiotics (oral erythromycin 250 mg QID for 10 days). Co-amoxiclav is contraindicated due to increased neonatal NEC risk (ORACLE I). Magnesium sulfate neuroprotection is given if delivery anticipated within 24 hours and gestation <30 weeks. Tocolysis is not recommended in PPROM. Monitoring includes maternal observations 4-hourly, FBC and CRP twice weekly, CTG daily and biophysical profile twice weekly. Delivery is recommended at 34 weeks or if chorioamnionitis, placental abruption, non-reassuring foetal status, or labour develops. Group B Strep prophylaxis in labour is mandatory unless screening negative within preceding 5 weeks.
key_points: Betamethasone 11.4 mg IM 24 hours apart x 2 doses
Erythromycin 250 mg QID PO x 10 days (NOT co-amoxiclav)
Magnesium neuroprotection if delivery <24 hours and <30 weeks
Avoid tocolysis in PPROM
Plan delivery at 34 weeks unless complications
explanation.pearls: ORACLE I showed co-amoxiclav in PPROM increased neonatal NEC by absolute 1.5%, which has made erythromycin the only recommended latency antibiotic.
